_meta.authoringNotes: Definitions per ICD-11 chronic pain classification (Treede RD et al. Pain 2019, PMID 30586068) and IASP. Epidemiology per global burden of disease chronic pain estimates and the US CDC chronic pain surveillance (Yong RJ et al. Pain 2022). Treatment principles per the CDC 2022 Clinical Practice Guideline for Prescribing Opioids for Pain (Dowell D et al. MMWR Recomm Rep 2022, PMID 36327391), NICE NG193 chronic primary pain (2021), and multidisciplinary pain rehabilitation evidence (Kamper SJ et al. Cochrane / BMJ 2015). Psychological therapy evidence per Williams ACC et al. Cochrane 2020 (PMID 32794606). Central sensitization mechanism per Woolf CJ. Pain 2011 (PMID 21251756).

h1Title: Chronic Pain Syndrome: Symptoms, Causes, Diagnosis, and Treatment
heroOverview: Chronic pain syndrome is persistent or recurrent pain lasting longer than three months that is accompanied by significant emotional distress and functional disability out of proportion to any identifiable tissue damage. It affects an estimated 20% of adults worldwide and roughly 50 million adults in the United States, making it one of the most common and costly health problems globally. Under the ICD-11 framework, chronic pain is recognized as a condition in its own right — chronic primary pain — when it cannot be better accounted for by another diagnosis, alongside chronic secondary pain syndromes linked to an underlying disease. The driving mechanism is central sensitization: amplified, self-perpetuating processing of pain signals in the spinal cord and brain, reinforced by sleep disruption, low mood, fear of movement, and pain catastrophizing. Treatment has shifted away from escalating opioids and toward a biopsychosocial model: multidisciplinary rehabilitation combining graded physical activity, psychological therapies such as cognitive behavioural therapy and acceptance and commitment therapy, sleep and mood treatment, carefully selected medications, and self-management education. The goal is restored function and quality of life rather than complete elimination of pain.

keyStats.prevalence: Affects roughly 20% of adults globally; about 50 million US adults have chronic pain and 17 million have high-impact chronic pain (CDC)
keyStats.demographics: More common in women, older adults, people with lower income and education, and those with coexisting mental health conditions
keyStats.avgAge: Prevalence rises with age but high-impact chronic pain affects all adult age groups
keyStats.globalCases: One of the leading causes of years lived with disability worldwide (Global Burden of Disease)

definition: Chronic pain syndrome (ICD-11: chronic primary pain MG30.0, and chronic secondary pain syndromes; ICD-10: G89 and R52) is pain that persists or recurs for more than three months and is associated with significant emotional distress (anxiety, anger, frustration, depressed mood) or functional disability (interference with daily activities and social roles). The ICD-11, adopted in 2022, was the first international classification to recognize chronic pain as a health condition in its own right. It distinguishes chronic primary pain — pain that is itself the disease, not explained by another condition, such as chronic widespread pain, chronic primary low back pain, and chronic primary headache — from chronic secondary pain, where pain is a symptom of an identifiable underlying disorder (cancer, surgery, trauma, neuropathy, musculoskeletal or visceral disease). The unifying biological mechanism across chronic pain is central sensitization: persistent nociceptive input, combined with genetic predisposition, psychological state, and environmental factors, produces lasting amplification of pain signaling in the dorsal horn and brain, loss of descending inhibitory control, and altered cortical processing. Pain then becomes decoupled from ongoing tissue damage. The biopsychosocial model recognizes that biological, psychological, and social factors all shape the experience and impact of chronic pain. The condition is managed by pain medicine, primary care, rehabilitation medicine, psychiatry, clinical psychology, and physiotherapy, ideally within an integrated multidisciplinary service.

typesClassification.description: Pain that is the condition itself, not better explained by another diagnosis. Includes chronic widespread pain (overlapping with fibromyalgia), chronic primary low back pain, chronic primary headache and orofacial pain, chronic primary visceral pain, and complex regional pain syndrome.
Persistent pain arising from an identified musculoskeletal disease such as osteoarthritis, rheumatoid arthritis, or persistent post-surgical or post-traumatic pain.
Persistent pain from internal organ disease — for example chronic pancreatitis, inflammatory bowel disease, endometriosis, or chronic pelvic pain.

Pain from a lesion or disease of the somatosensory nervous system, either peripheral (diabetic neuropathy, post-herpetic neuralgia, radiculopathy) or central (post-stroke pain, spinal cord injury pain).
Pain caused by cancer itself or by cancer treatment (surgery, chemotherapy-induced neuropathy, radiation). Managed within palliative and oncology pain services.
Pain persisting beyond the expected healing period after surgery or injury, occurring in 10-50% of certain operations and a frequent cause of long-term disability.

A clinically important subgroup defined by chronic pain that substantially restricts work, social, and self-care activities; it carries the heaviest burden of disability, mental health comorbidity, and healthcare use.

primarySymptoms: Persistent or recurrent pain lasting more than three months, which may be constant or fluctuating, and is often described as aching, burning, throbbing, or a deep dragging discomfort.
Pain intensity and disability that are out of proportion to any demonstrable tissue damage on examination or imaging.
Marked interference with work, household tasks, social activities, exercise, and sleep.
Disturbed, unrefreshing sleep, with difficulty falling asleep and frequent waking, which in turn worsens pain the next day.
Persistent fatigue and reduced physical stamina.
Low mood, irritability, anxiety, frustration, and a sense of hopelessness about the pain.
Pain catastrophizing — rumination about pain, magnification of its threat, and a feeling of helplessness — which strongly predicts worse outcomes.
Fear of movement (kinesiophobia) leading to activity avoidance, deconditioning, and a downward spiral of worsening function.
Cognitive difficulties — poor concentration, forgetfulness, and slowed thinking, sometimes called 'pain fog'.
Widespread tenderness and heightened sensitivity to pressure, temperature, light, and sound in patients with prominent central sensitization.
Reduced participation in valued life roles, social withdrawal, and strained relationships.
earlyWarningSigns: Pain from an injury or illness that is not settling within the expected healing time of 6-12 weeks
Increasing reliance on rest and activity avoidance because of fear that movement will cause damage
Sleep that is becoming disrupted by pain, with daytime fatigue
Low mood, irritability, or anxiety developing alongside persistent pain
Escalating use of analgesics, including over-the-counter and opioid medication, with diminishing benefit
emergencySigns: New severe pain with fever, unexplained weight loss, or night pain — exclude infection or malignancy (red-flag features)
New neurological deficit — limb weakness, numbness, or bowel or bladder dysfunction — exclude cord compression or cauda equina syndrome
Suicidal thoughts or plans related to unrelenting pain — requires urgent mental health assessment
Signs of opioid overdose — sedation, confusion, slow or shallow breathing — a medical emergency
Acute severe worsening of chronic pain with systemic illness — needs prompt medical evaluation

causes.cause: Central sensitization and altered pain processing
causes.description: Persistent nociceptive input drives lasting amplification of pain signaling in the spinal dorsal horn and brain, loss of descending inhibitory control, and cortical reorganization. Pain becomes self-sustaining and decoupled from tissue damage — the core mechanism of chronic primary pain.

causes.cause: An initiating injury, surgery, or illness
causes.description: Many chronic pain syndromes begin with an identifiable trigger — a back injury, an operation, a fracture, an episode of shingles, or an inflammatory illness — that fails to resolve normally and transitions to persistent pain.

Osteoarthritis, rheumatoid arthritis, diabetic neuropathy, endometriosis, inflammatory bowel disease, cancer, and many other conditions generate ongoing nociceptive or neuropathic input that becomes chronic pain.

Depression, anxiety, post-traumatic stress, pain catastrophizing, fear-avoidance behaviour, and a history of adverse childhood experiences all increase the risk that acute pain becomes chronic and that chronic pain becomes disabling.
Poor and unrefreshing sleep both results from and worsens chronic pain, lowering pain thresholds and impairing the body's natural pain modulation — a bidirectional vicious cycle.

Twin and family studies show heritable contributions to pain sensitivity and the risk of chronic pain. Female sex, sex hormones, and individual differences in nociceptive and immune signaling all influence susceptibility.

Low income, job strain, lack of social support, work that involves heavy physical demands, and limited access to healthcare are associated with higher rates of chronic pain and greater disability from it.

riskFactors.description: Chronic pain, and especially chronic widespread pain, is consistently more common in women across populations.
Prevalence of chronic pain rises with age, although high-impact chronic pain affects all adult age groups.
Mood disorders both predispose to chronic pain and worsen its impact; they are among the strongest modifiable predictors of poor outcome.
Catastrophic thinking about pain and avoidance of activity strongly predict the transition from acute to chronic disabling pain and are key targets for psychological therapy.
Poor sleep lowers pain thresholds and impairs natural pain modulation; treating it is an essential part of chronic pain care.

riskFactors.factor: Prior injury, surgery, or chronic disease
riskFactors.description: Persistent post-surgical pain occurs after 10-50% of certain operations; chronic diseases such as arthritis and diabetes are common sources of chronic secondary pain.

riskFactors.factor: Lower socioeconomic status and adverse life circumstances
riskFactors.description: Low income, job strain, social isolation, and adverse childhood experiences are associated with higher chronic pain prevalence and disability.
Prolonged opioid therapy can worsen pain through opioid-induced hyperalgesia and contributes to disability, tolerance, and dependence.

affectedDemographics: Globally, chronic pain affects roughly one in five adults and is a leading cause of years lived with disability.
In the United States, approximately 50 million adults have chronic pain and about 17 million have high-impact chronic pain that limits major life activities.
Women are affected more often than men across nearly all chronic pain conditions.
Prevalence is higher among adults with lower income and education, those in physically demanding occupations, and those with coexisting mental health conditions.
Older adults have the highest overall prevalence, but high-impact chronic pain causes substantial disability in working-age adults, with major economic consequences.
diagnosisOverview: Chronic pain syndrome is a clinical diagnosis. The first task is a careful history and examination to identify or exclude a specific treatable cause, screen for red-flag features (fever, unexplained weight loss, night pain, neurological deficit, history of cancer, immunosuppression), and characterize the pain — its site, quality, time course, and the factors that worsen and relieve it. Targeted investigations (blood tests, imaging, nerve conduction studies) are ordered only when the history and examination suggest a specific diagnosis; indiscriminate imaging in chronic primary pain often reveals incidental findings that lead to harm. Once dangerous causes are excluded, assessment shifts to the biopsychosocial picture: validated tools quantify pain intensity and interference (Brief Pain Inventory, pain numeric rating scale), mood (PHQ-9 for depression, GAD-7 for anxiety), sleep, pain catastrophizing (Pain Catastrophizing Scale), fear of movement, and overall function and quality of life. The ICD-11 framework guides classification into chronic primary pain or a chronic secondary pain syndrome. Identifying coexisting depression, anxiety, post-traumatic stress, substance use, and sleep disorder is essential because these strongly shape treatment and prognosis. A current medication review — including over-the-counter analgesics and opioids — assesses for medication overuse, opioid-induced hyperalgesia, and dependence. The diagnostic process itself is therapeutic when it is used to validate the patient's experience, explain central sensitization in accessible terms, set realistic functional goals, and move the focus from a search for a structural 'fix' toward active self-management.

diagnosticTests.test: Comprehensive pain history and physical examination with red-flag screening
diagnosticTests.purpose: Identifies or excludes a specific treatable cause and screens for serious pathology (infection, malignancy, neurological compromise)
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A detailed consultation lasting 30-60 minutes covering the pain, its impact, mental health, sleep, medications, and a focused physical examination.

diagnosticTests.test: Validated pain and function questionnaires (Brief Pain Inventory, pain interference scales)
diagnosticTests.purpose: Quantifies pain intensity, interference with daily life, and disability to guide treatment and track progress
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Short self-completed questionnaires done in the clinic or at home; repeated over time to measure response.

diagnosticTests.test: Mood, sleep, and catastrophizing screening (PHQ-9, GAD-7, Pain Catastrophizing Scale)
diagnosticTests.purpose: Detects depression, anxiety, sleep disturbance, and catastrophic thinking — strong predictors of outcome and key treatment targets
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Brief validated questionnaires completed during assessment.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Used selectively when history suggests inflammatory, metabolic, or systemic disease (for example ESR/CRP, full blood count, thyroid function, HbA1c, vitamin D)
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A standard blood draw; results within 24-72 hours. Not routinely needed in chronic primary pain.

diagnosticTests.test: Targeted imaging or nerve conduction studies
diagnosticTests.purpose: Reserved for cases where the history and examination point to a specific structural or neuropathic cause
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Performed selectively; imaging in chronic primary pain is generally avoided because incidental findings can lead to unnecessary intervention.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Identifies medication overuse, opioid-induced hyperalgesia, dependence, and drug interactions
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A structured review of all current analgesics including over-the-counter products and opioids, done during the consultation.

treatmentOverview: Modern management of chronic pain syndrome is built on the biopsychosocial model and aims to restore function, mood, sleep, and quality of life rather than to eliminate pain completely. The foundation is multidisciplinary, non-pharmacological treatment. Graded physical activity and exercise — tailored, paced, and progressed gradually with a physiotherapist — reverse deconditioning and reduce pain and disability across most chronic pain conditions; the specific type matters less than consistency. Psychological therapies are core, not optional: cognitive behavioural therapy and acceptance and commitment therapy reduce pain-related distress, catastrophizing, and disability, and pain neuroscience education helps patients understand central sensitization and reduces fear of movement. Sleep is actively treated, usually with cognitive behavioural therapy for insomnia. Coexisting depression and anxiety are treated in their own right. Self-management support — pacing, goal-setting, flare planning, and return-to-work or return-to-activity planning — is emphasized throughout. Pharmacological treatment is an adjunct, selected by pain type. For chronic primary pain, NICE NG193 recommends against starting most analgesics, including opioids, paracetamol, and NSAIDs, and instead supports a trial of an antidepressant (such as duloxetine or amitriptyline) alongside the non-drug measures above. For chronic neuropathic pain, first-line drugs are tricyclic antidepressants, duloxetine, and gabapentinoids. For chronic secondary pain from inflammatory or musculoskeletal disease, treatment of the underlying condition and judicious use of NSAIDs or other agents are appropriate. Opioids have a limited role: the 2022 CDC guideline emphasizes that they are not first-line for chronic pain, that benefits for long-term chronic pain are limited and risks substantial, and that any use should involve the lowest effective dose, clear functional goals, regular review, and consideration of tapering when harms outweigh benefits. Interventional procedures (nerve blocks, radiofrequency ablation, spinal cord stimulation) have a defined but limited role for specific conditions and are not a substitute for the rehabilitative core of treatment. Intensive multidisciplinary pain rehabilitation programs are the most effective option for high-impact, refractory chronic pain.

medicalTreatments.name: Graded exercise and physical rehabilitation
medicalTreatments.description: Individually tailored, paced, progressively increased physical activity delivered by a physiotherapist. Reverses deconditioning, reduces fear of movement, and improves pain and function across chronic pain conditions.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Consistently reduces pain and disability across chronic pain populations in systematic reviews; the cornerstone of treatment

medicalTreatments.name: Cognitive behavioural therapy and acceptance and commitment therapy
medicalTreatments.description: Structured psychological therapies that reduce pain catastrophizing, fear-avoidance, distress, and disability and improve mood and self-efficacy. Delivered individually, in groups, or digitally.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Small-to-moderate but reliable improvements in disability, distress, and catastrophizing (Williams Cochrane 2020, PMID 32794606)

medicalTreatments.name: Duloxetine 30-120 mg orally daily
medicalTreatments.description: An SNRI antidepressant that treats chronic primary pain (recommended by NICE NG193), chronic musculoskeletal pain, and neuropathic pain, and also treats coexisting depression and anxiety.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Modest reduction in pain and improvement in function; number-needed-to-treat around 6 in neuropathic pain

medicalTreatments.name: Tricyclic antidepressants (amitriptyline, nortriptyline) 10-75 mg at night
medicalTreatments.description: Low-dose tricyclics improve sleep and reduce neuropathic and chronic primary pain. Side effects include sedation, dry mouth, and constipation; used cautiously in older adults.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Number-needed-to-treat around 3-4 for 50% pain relief in neuropathic pain; also improves sleep

medicalTreatments.name: Gabapentinoids (gabapentin, pregabalin) for neuropathic pain
medicalTreatments.description: First-line for chronic neuropathic pain components. Titrated slowly; side effects include dizziness, sedation, and weight gain. Not recommended for non-neuropathic chronic primary pain.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Number-needed-to-treat around 6-8 for 50% relief in neuropathic pain

medicalTreatments.name: Cognitive behavioural therapy for insomnia (CBT-I)
medicalTreatments.description: Structured behavioural treatment of the sleep disturbance that perpetuates chronic pain. First-line for chronic insomnia and preferred over long-term hypnotics.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Substantial, durable improvement in sleep quality, with secondary benefit for pain and mood

medicalTreatments.name: Judicious, time-limited NSAID or paracetamol use for chronic secondary pain
medicalTreatments.description: Appropriate for flares of chronic secondary musculoskeletal pain at the lowest effective dose for the shortest period, with attention to gastrointestinal, renal, and cardiovascular risk.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Modest short-term benefit in chronic secondary musculoskeletal pain; not recommended as ongoing treatment for chronic primary pain

medicalTreatments.name: Opioids — limited, carefully managed role
medicalTreatments.description: Not first-line for chronic non-cancer pain. If considered, the CDC 2022 guideline advises the lowest effective dose, explicit functional goals, regular review, avoidance of concurrent benzodiazepines, and consideration of tapering when harms outweigh benefits.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Limited evidence of long-term benefit in chronic pain; substantial risk of tolerance, dependence, hyperalgesia, and overdose

surgicalOptions.name: Spinal cord stimulation
surgicalOptions.description: An implanted device delivering electrical stimulation to the dorsal columns, used for selected refractory neuropathic and post-surgical pain after multidisciplinary assessment and a successful trial period.
surgicalOptions.successRate: Meaningful pain relief in roughly 50-60% of carefully selected patients at 1-2 years; effectiveness in non-neuropathic chronic primary pain is not established

surgicalOptions.name: Targeted interventional procedures (nerve blocks, radiofrequency ablation, epidural injections)
surgicalOptions.description: Image-guided procedures used for specific, well-defined pain generators (for example facet joint pain or radicular pain). They are adjuncts within a rehabilitation plan, not stand-alone cures.
surgicalOptions.successRate: Variable and condition-specific; benefit is often temporary and best used to enable participation in rehabilitation

surgicalOptions.name: Intrathecal drug delivery
surgicalOptions.description: An implanted pump delivering analgesic medication directly into the cerebrospinal fluid, reserved for refractory cancer pain and selected severe non-cancer pain after specialist multidisciplinary review.
surgicalOptions.successRate: Symptom improvement in selected refractory patients; requires ongoing specialist management

alternativeTreatments.name: Mindfulness-based stress reduction and mindfulness-based cognitive therapy
alternativeTreatments.description: Structured mindfulness programs reduce pain-related distress and improve coping and quality of life, with evidence comparable to other psychological therapies for chronic pain.

alternativeTreatments.name: Acupuncture and physical modalities (TENS, heat, manual therapy)
alternativeTreatments.description: May provide short-term symptomatic relief for some patients and can support engagement in active rehabilitation; effects are generally modest and best used as adjuncts rather than primary treatment.

whySeeSpecialist: Most chronic pain is managed in primary care, but referral to a multidisciplinary pain medicine service is warranted for high-impact chronic pain, diagnostic uncertainty after red-flag exclusion, failure of initial treatment, complex medication issues including opioid dependence, and when intensive rehabilitation or interventional procedures are being considered. Specialist services coordinate the physical, psychological, and pharmacological components that single-discipline care cannot.
doctorSelectionGuide: Choose a pain medicine service that works as a genuine multidisciplinary team — physician, physiotherapist, clinical psychologist, and often occupational therapist and nurse — and that emphasizes function and self-management. Be cautious of clinics that rely primarily on repeated injections or escalating opioid prescriptions without a rehabilitative framework.
hospitalCriteria: Multidisciplinary pain medicine service with medical, physiotherapy, and psychology staffing
Access to intensive multidisciplinary pain rehabilitation programs
Structured opioid stewardship and tapering support
Mental health services for coexisting depression, anxiety, and trauma
Interventional pain capability for appropriately selected patients
keyFacilities: Clinical psychology and pain-focused mental health services
Primary care practices with chronic pain pathways
preventionStrategies: Treat acute pain effectively and encourage early return to normal activity rather than prolonged rest, which reduces the risk of transition to chronic pain.
Identify and address psychological risk factors — catastrophizing, fear-avoidance, low mood — early after an injury or surgery.
Optimize perioperative pain management to lower the risk of persistent post-surgical pain.
Avoid starting long-term opioids for acute or subacute pain, which is a major driver of later dependence and disability.
Maintain regular physical activity, healthy sleep, and good mental health, all of which protect against chronic pain.
Provide clear reassurance and education to patients with acute pain to counter unhelpful beliefs that movement causes damage.
lifestyleModifications: Stay active and use pacing — break activities into manageable parts with planned rest — rather than alternating overactivity and prolonged rest.
Build and maintain a consistent exercise routine even on higher-pain days, adjusting intensity rather than stopping.
Prioritize regular, good-quality sleep with a consistent routine.
Practice relaxation, mindfulness, or other psychological coping strategies daily.
Set specific, realistic functional goals (such as a valued activity or return to work) and track progress toward them.
Limit alcohol, avoid smoking, and review analgesic use regularly with a clinician to prevent medication overuse.

recommended: A balanced, predominantly anti-inflammatory dietary pattern (vegetables, fruit, whole grains, fish, olive oil)
Adequate protein and overall nutrition to support physical reconditioning
Adequate fiber and fluids to manage constipation from pain medications
Regular meals to stabilize energy and support mood and sleep
avoid: Excess alcohol, which disrupts sleep and interacts with pain medications
Highly processed, high-sugar diets associated with weight gain and worse pain outcomes
Using food restriction or fad diets as a substitute for evidence-based pain treatment
Excess caffeine late in the day, which worsens the sleep disturbance that perpetuates pain

exerciseGuidelines: Graded, paced physical activity is the single most effective treatment for chronic pain. Work with a physiotherapist to set a sustainable baseline and increase gradually. The type of exercise matters less than consistency — walking, swimming, cycling, strength work, yoga, and tai chi are all effective. Expect some increase in symptoms when starting, which settles; this is not a sign of harm. Avoid the boom-and-bust cycle of overdoing activity then resting completely.
dailyManagement: Follow a consistent daily routine of paced activity, exercise, and rest.
Take prescribed medications regularly as directed rather than only at peak pain.
Keep a brief diary of activity, sleep, mood, and pain to identify patterns and track progress.
Practice a daily relaxation, mindfulness, or breathing routine.
Maintain social contact and valued activities, adapting rather than abandoning them.
Have a written flare plan and use it early when pain increases, rather than reverting to bed rest.
prognosis: Chronic pain syndrome is typically a long-term condition, and the prognosis depends heavily on how it is managed. Complete resolution of pain is uncommon once pain has been present for many months, but most people can achieve substantial improvement in function, mood, sleep, and quality of life with a biopsychosocial, multidisciplinary approach. Outcomes are best when treatment begins early, when depression, anxiety, sleep disturbance, and catastrophizing are actively addressed, when the patient is engaged in graded activity and self-management, and when long-term opioid use is avoided or carefully managed. High-impact chronic pain carries the worst prognosis for disability and is associated with higher rates of mental health comorbidity, unemployment, and healthcare use, but even this group benefits meaningfully from intensive multidisciplinary rehabilitation. Conversely, a purely biomedical approach focused on repeated investigations, escalating analgesics, and a search for a structural 'fix' tends to entrench disability. Realistic goal-setting — measuring success by what a person can do, not by the pain score alone — and a shift from cure to confident self-management is associated with the best long-term outcomes and quality of life.
recoveryTimeline: Chronic pain syndrome does not follow an acute recovery curve. Psychological therapies and graded exercise produce gradual gains over weeks to months. Antidepressant trials for pain take 6-8 weeks at adequate dose. Intensive multidisciplinary rehabilitation programs typically run over several weeks with continued benefit over the following year. Sleep usually improves within weeks of starting cognitive behavioural therapy for insomnia. Improvement is measured in function and quality of life over months, not in rapid elimination of pain.
complications: Progressive physical deconditioning and loss of function from activity avoidance.
Depression, anxiety, and elevated suicide risk associated with persistent disabling pain.
Sleep disorder that perpetuates and amplifies pain.
Opioid-related harm — tolerance, dependence, opioid-induced hyperalgesia, and overdose — from escalating long-term use.
Medication overuse and adverse effects from analgesics, including gastrointestinal, renal, and cardiovascular harm from NSAIDs.
Loss of employment, financial hardship, and social isolation.
Iatrogenic harm from unnecessary investigations and procedures driven by a purely biomedical approach.

supportResources.name: International Association for the Study of Pain (IASP)
supportResources.description: Global professional body publishing the chronic pain classification, education, and treatment guidance.

supportResources.name: American Chronic Pain Association
supportResources.description: Patient-led organisation providing self-management resources and peer support for people with chronic pain.

supportResources.name: Pain Concern (UK)
supportResources.description: UK charity offering information, a helpline, and self-management resources for people living with chronic pain.

supportResources.name: US Pain Foundation
supportResources.description: Patient advocacy and education organisation supporting people with chronic pain conditions.

confusedWithConditions.name: Fibromyalgia
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Fibromyalgia is a specific chronic primary pain condition defined by chronic widespread pain with fatigue, sleep disturbance, and cognitive symptoms. Chronic pain syndrome is the broader umbrella; fibromyalgia is one diagnosis within it.

confusedWithConditions.name: Central Pain Syndrome
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Central pain syndrome is caused by a confirmed lesion of the brain or spinal cord (stroke, MS, spinal cord injury). Chronic pain syndrome, especially chronic primary pain, is not attributable to a single structural lesion.

confusedWithConditions.name: Depression
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Depression frequently coexists with chronic pain and shares fatigue, sleep disturbance, and reduced function. The two are distinguished by the primacy and pattern of symptoms, and both often need treatment together; depression alone lacks the persistent physical pain that defines chronic pain syndrome.

confusedWithConditions.name: Chronic Fatigue Syndrome
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: ME/CFS is defined by post-exertional malaise and disabling fatigue as the cardinal features. Chronic pain syndrome is defined by persistent pain. The two overlap and can coexist, but the dominant symptom differs.

relatedConditions.name: Fibromyalgia
relatedConditions.relevance: A specific chronic primary pain condition within the chronic pain syndrome umbrella, sharing central sensitization and biopsychosocial management.

relatedConditions.name: Central Pain Syndrome
relatedConditions.relevance: A chronic neuropathic pain syndrome with shared multidisciplinary treatment principles.

relatedConditions.name: Neuropathic Pain
relatedConditions.relevance: A major category of chronic pain with distinct first-line pharmacotherapy.

relatedConditions.name: Back Pain
relatedConditions.relevance: Chronic primary low back pain is one of the most common chronic pain presentations worldwide.

relatedConditions.name: Chronic Low Back Pain
relatedConditions.relevance: A frequent, high-burden chronic pain syndrome managed with the same biopsychosocial framework.

faqs.question: What is chronic pain syndrome?
faqs.answer: Chronic pain syndrome is persistent or recurrent pain lasting more than three months that is accompanied by significant emotional distress and functional disability, often out of proportion to identifiable tissue damage. It is recognized by the ICD-11 as a health condition in its own right.

faqs.question: What is the difference between chronic primary and chronic secondary pain?
faqs.answer: Chronic primary pain is pain that is itself the disease and cannot be better explained by another condition, such as chronic widespread pain or chronic primary low back pain. Chronic secondary pain is a symptom of an identifiable underlying disorder such as cancer, arthritis, or neuropathy.

faqs.question: What causes chronic pain to persist?
faqs.answer: The main mechanism is central sensitization — lasting amplification of pain signaling in the spinal cord and brain, with loss of natural pain-dampening control. Sleep disruption, low mood, fear of movement, and pain catastrophizing reinforce the cycle, so pain becomes decoupled from tissue damage.

faqs.question: Can chronic pain syndrome be cured?
faqs.answer: Complete cure is uncommon once pain has lasted many months, but most people achieve major improvements in function, mood, sleep, and quality of life with multidisciplinary treatment. The goal shifts from eliminating pain to restoring a full and active life despite some ongoing pain.

faqs.question: How is chronic pain syndrome treated?
faqs.answer: Treatment is multidisciplinary and based on the biopsychosocial model: graded exercise, psychological therapies such as cognitive behavioural therapy, sleep and mood treatment, self-management education, and carefully selected medication. Opioids are not first-line. Intensive pain rehabilitation programs help refractory cases.

faqs.question: Are opioids good for chronic pain?
faqs.answer: Opioids are not first-line for chronic non-cancer pain. Long-term benefit is limited and the risks of tolerance, dependence, opioid-induced hyperalgesia, and overdose are substantial. The CDC 2022 guideline advises using the lowest effective dose with clear functional goals and considering tapering when harms outweigh benefits.

faqs.question: Does exercise help or harm chronic pain?
faqs.answer: Graded, paced exercise is the single most effective treatment for chronic pain. A temporary increase in symptoms when starting is expected and is not a sign of harm. The type of exercise matters less than consistency, and avoiding activity worsens deconditioning and pain over time.

faqs.question: Why is psychological therapy used for a physical pain problem?
faqs.answer: Chronic pain is processed in the brain, and psychological factors such as catastrophizing, fear of movement, low mood, and poor sleep strongly influence pain intensity and disability. Therapies like cognitive behavioural therapy address these factors, reducing distress and improving function — they treat the pain experience, not imaginary pain.

faqs.question: What is pain catastrophizing?
faqs.answer: Pain catastrophizing is a pattern of rumination about pain, magnification of its threat, and a feeling of helplessness. It is one of the strongest predictors of the transition from acute to chronic disabling pain and is a key target of psychological therapy.

faqs.question: Is chronic pain syndrome a mental health condition?
faqs.answer: No. Chronic pain syndrome is a real physical condition involving changes in the nervous system. However, it strongly interacts with mental health — depression, anxiety, and sleep disturbance both worsen pain and are worsened by it — so treating mental health alongside the pain is essential.

faqs.question: How common is chronic pain?
faqs.answer: Chronic pain affects roughly one in five adults worldwide. In the United States about 50 million adults have chronic pain and around 17 million have high-impact chronic pain that substantially limits daily activities. It is a leading cause of disability globally.

faqs.question: Why do scans often look normal in chronic pain syndrome?
faqs.answer: In chronic primary pain, pain is generated and amplified by changes in the nervous system rather than by ongoing structural damage, so imaging is often normal or shows only incidental age-related findings. This is why indiscriminate scanning is discouraged — it can lead to unnecessary and harmful interventions.

faqs.question: Should I rest or stay active with chronic pain?
faqs.answer: Staying active in a paced, graded way is better than prolonged rest. Rest beyond the early days of an acute injury leads to deconditioning, fear of movement, and worse pain. Pacing — balancing activity and rest and increasing gradually — is the recommended approach.

faqs.question: Can chronic pain syndrome cause depression?
faqs.answer: Yes. Persistent disabling pain commonly leads to depression and anxiety, and these in turn intensify pain and reduce function — a bidirectional relationship. This is why screening for and treating mood and sleep disorders is a core part of chronic pain care.

faqs.question: What is high-impact chronic pain?
faqs.answer: High-impact chronic pain is chronic pain that substantially restricts work, social, and self-care activities. It affects around 17 million US adults, carries the heaviest burden of disability and mental health comorbidity, and benefits most from intensive multidisciplinary rehabilitation.

faqs.question: Do antidepressants work for chronic pain even without depression?
faqs.answer: Yes. Certain antidepressants — particularly duloxetine and low-dose tricyclics such as amitriptyline — reduce pain through effects on pain-modulating pathways, independent of their effect on mood. They are recommended for chronic primary pain and chronic neuropathic pain.

faqs.question: How long does treatment take to work?
faqs.answer: Chronic pain treatment works gradually. Psychological therapy and graded exercise produce gains over weeks to months. Antidepressant pain trials take 6-8 weeks at adequate dose. Intensive rehabilitation programs run over several weeks with continued benefit over the following year. Progress is measured in function, not rapid pain elimination.

faqs.question: Is chronic pain syndrome the same as fibromyalgia?
faqs.answer: No, but they are related. Fibromyalgia is one specific chronic primary pain condition, defined by chronic widespread pain with fatigue and sleep and cognitive symptoms. Chronic pain syndrome is the broader umbrella covering many chronic pain conditions, including fibromyalgia.

faqs.question: When should I see a pain specialist?
faqs.answer: See a multidisciplinary pain service for high-impact chronic pain, when initial treatment fails, when there is diagnostic uncertainty after serious causes are excluded, for complex medication problems including opioid dependence, or when intensive rehabilitation or interventional procedures are being considered.

faqs.question: Can chronic pain be prevented?
faqs.answer: Risk can be reduced. Treating acute pain well, encouraging early return to activity, addressing psychological risk factors early, optimizing pain control around surgery, and avoiding long-term opioids for acute pain all lower the chance that acute pain becomes chronic disabling pain.

symptomKeywords: pain not improving despite normal scans
pain interfering with work and sleep
metaTitle: Chronic Pain Syndrome: Symptoms & Treatment | aihealz
metaDescription: Chronic pain syndrome: persistent pain with distress and disability. Central sensitization, biopsychosocial treatment, exercise and therapy reviewed.
